attributes.stem: A 67-year-old man presents to the Emergency department with an eight hour history of constant left lower quadrant pain. He has no other gastrointestinal no genitourinary symptoms. He is otherwise in good health, apart from hypertension, for which he takes perindopril. His blood pressure is 132/76 mmHg, pulse 70/min and temperature 37.8 °C. His abdomen is soft, apart from some tenderness in the left iliac fossa. His examination is otherwise unremarkable. His stool is negative for occult blood. Urinalysis reveals no leukocytes or RBCs, and Gram stain is negative. Laboratory tests reveal a leukocyte count of 11 x 10^9 cells/L and normal electrolytes and renal function. A CT scan is performed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following changes seen on the CT would be most indicative of the necessity of an emergency admission for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
The patient presents with a clinical picture of acute diverticulitis-fever. He is relatively well apart from a low grade fever and mild abdominal tenderness. For such patients a good case can be made for further management on an out-patient basis. Provided the patient has adequate home support he and the family can be briefed on home support, possibly given a course of antibiotics, arrangements made for follow-up with his general practitioner or an outpatient clinic and instructions to return if his condition worsens.

A CT scan will be very helpful in confirming the diagnosis and seeing if there are any changes that would merit immediate hospitalisation.

The presence of a pericolic abscess larger than 6 cm on CT would suggest the necessity of admitting this patient to the hospital due to a high risk of complications. A pericolic abscess is a common complication of diverticulitis and small abscesses can be managed conservatively. If the abscess is large and/or has a thick wall, there is a higher risk of rupture or fistula formation, which can lead to peritonitis or sepsis. Therefore, admission to the hospital for antibiotic therapy and possible drainage is recommended.

Fat stranding refers to an abnormal thickening and increased density of the fat that surrounds the organs in the abdomen. This can be a sign of inflammation or infection in the area and is a common CT finding in acute diverticulitis. Treatment would depend on the underlying cause, but may include antibiotics, anti-inflammatory medications, and supportive measures such as bowel rest and pain management.

Mucosal oedema refers to swelling of the lining of the intestines. This can be a sign of inflammation, infection, or other types of injury and is a common radiological observation in diverticulitis.

A fistula is an abnormal connection between two organs or between an organ and the skin. In this case, fistula formation could be suggestive of an abnormal connection between the colon and another structure, such as the bladder, which could be causing the patient's symptoms. Treatment would depend on the location and severity of the fistula, but may include antibiotics, surgical repair, or other interventions such as drainage or stenting. This would not necessarily need to be done in an emergency setting.

Free fluid in the pelvis can be a normal finding in the context of diverticulitis, and may indicate inflammation or abscess formation. However, the presence of free fluid alone does not necessarily indicate a need for hospitalisation, as the severity and location of the inflammation or abscess will determine the appropriate treatment.

Other indications for immediate hospitalisation would include the presence of free gas on the CT scan - suggesting perforation.
learningPoints: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen is the recommended imaging modality for confirming the diagnosis of acute diverticulitis.
If the abscess is large and/or has a thick wall, there is a higher risk of rupture or fistula formation, which can lead to peritonitis or sepsis.
references: Linzay CD, Pandit S. Acute Diverticulitis. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.
